input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tooling and enablers include an MBSE framework that uses SysML for architectural definition, UML for object modelling, and MontiArc for component‑level behaviour; development is supported by the MathWorks Simulink simulation engine; an Asset Administration Shell (AAS) encapsulates sensor and actuator data and provides a standard interface; and MQTT is used for real‑time data exchange between the physical system and the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Sensor data streams are transmitted from the physical machine to the digital shadow in a real‑time, continuous fashion over MQTT. The system processes each incoming time‑series message immediately, thereby synchronizing the digital representation with the physical state at the moment of arrival; explicit synchronization frequencies or latency thresholds are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin is organized using a layered reference model that merges MBSE practices. Functional and logical architecture is captured with SysML IBDs and ADLs, while the virtual model is built and validated through simulation in Simulink. The overall orchestration follows a concrete workflow in which sensor data traces are aggregated, processed by an active processing layer, and used to update a virtual asset model; communication and resource coordination are mediated through an MQTT broker serving as the data bus, and the architecture relies on UML‑style class diagrams to capture component integration.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external equipment is achieved solely through the MQTT protocol, delivering raw sensor time series from a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D industrial controller to the digital twin. The data flow is strictly one‑way, as defined for a digital shadow; no commands or control data are sent back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin is assembled using Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE). A SysML model captures the system’s component‑connector architecture and functional topology, while a MathWorks Simulink model represents the dynamics of the permanent‑magnet synchronous motor, gear‑train and cycloidal gear assembly as well as their interactions with the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D SL servo drive. The Simulink model is linked to the SysML model through a model‑relationship interface, enabling the Twin to synchronize behavioral simulation with the structural taxonomy. Measurement data from the physical system is streamed in real time via MQTT topics published by an edge gateway; this telemetry is aggregated in a round‑robin database and fed back into the Simulink model for time‑aligned simulation. The overall constellation couples a behavioral Simulink model, a structural SysML taxonomy, and a real‑time MQTT data layer to maintain an up‑to‑date digital representation of the physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Twin controls a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor driven by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D SL servo drive. The motor is coupled to a gear train that reduces torque and delivers the output of a cycloidal gear assembly. Actuation commands are issued through MQTT messages (e.g., to topic 'twin/control/motor') which are received by the servo drive; the drive translates these into torque commands for the motor. Control capabilities include open‑loop velocity commands, closed‑loop position feedback via encoders, automatic limits on position and torque for safety, and emergency stop handling. The actuator’s addressing is performed over the MQTT topic hierarchy, and safety constraints are enforced by the Siemens controller’s built‑in protection logic.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Each drive is instrumented with two high‑resolution encoders: a motor encoder that measures shaft rotation and a secondary encoder on the output shaft, along with current sensors for the motor.  The encoder data and motor current are published to MQTT topics and consumed by the Digital Twin.  The dual encoder arrangement allows the cancellation of mechanical backlash and provides high‑accuracy position monitoring for the simulation.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Twin’s fidelity is evaluated by comparing simulated outputs from the Simulink model against measured telemetry from the physical system. Discrepancies are quantified using a root‑mean‑square error metric between simulated and real position/time data. Data uncertainty originates from sensor noise, encoder quantization, and MQTT network latency; the Twin tracks these uncertainties and flags parameters when error exceeds predefined thresholds, triggering a recalibration routine. No additional automated validation loops, fault detection algorithms, or periodic consistency checks are described beyond this error‑threshold monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time‑series data of all sensor streams (e.g., input current and output angular position) is transmitted from the physical asset to the digital twin through MQTT. The data is published at a configurable sampling rate (not specified in the document) and consumed by the digital twin application for reconstruction and visualization. No further preprocessing or aggregation is defined in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: No virtual‑to‑physical interaction is defined. The digital twin is a digital shadow that only observes and processes incoming sensor streams; it does not generate or send control commands, feedback, or any backward data to the physical robot. Consequently, there are no command types, control message formats, validation, authentication, conflict resolution or execution confirmation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The primary communication channel is an MQTT broker that receives telemetry from the physical asset and delivers it to the digital twin. Configuration details such as topic hierarchy, QoS levels, TLS, or network topology are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development of the digital twin follows a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach. System models are defined at multiple abstraction layers—requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers—using modeling languages such as SysML. The RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) methodology is applied to separate concerns, while SMArDT (Smart Model‑Based Evolved Digital Twins) guides the systematic construction of a smart twin. Architectures are expressed through component‑connector ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Interaction Block Diagrams (IBDs). Dynamic behaviour can be specified with MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines, subsequently simulated in MathWorks Simulink or equivalent simulation platforms. From simulation models, executable code can be derived in languages like C++ or Java. The document does not provide details on version‑control mechanisms, formal quality‑assurance practices, or evolution–management processes.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the system in Section III 'USE CASE'. It identifies the physical system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', its control system ('Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'), and its application ('machining'). Therefore, the claim that it is not in the document is incorrect.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text describes specific services provided by the digital shadow. These include 'visualization in a dashboard' and enabling the 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' to enhance motion accuracy. Claiming the information is missing ignores these explicit functional descriptions.

The extraction accurately identifies the tools and enablers mentioned in the text: MBSE with SysML and UML for modeling, MontiArc for architecture, Simulink for simulation, and MQTT for data streaming. However, the description of the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) includes unsupported details ('encapsulates sensor and actuator data and provides a standard interface') which are not present in the provided source text; the text only mentions 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' without defining their functionality.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the use of MBSE, SysML for structural modeling, and Simulink for behavioral simulation of the motor and gears, as well as the use of MQTT for data streaming. However, it adds unsupported details such as storage in a 'round-robin database' and a specific 'model-relationship interface,' and claims 'time-aligned simulation' which are not mentioned in the text. Quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink', 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.'

While the extraction correctly lists the physical components (motor, gear train, cycloidal gear) and the Siemens controller, it incorrectly describes the control mechanism. The text defines the system as a 'passive entity' with 'one-way data flow' (a Digital Shadow), whereas the extraction claims the Twin 'controls' the motor via MQTT commands (e.g., 'twin/control/motor'), implying active control which contradicts the source. Safety limits and E-stop handling via MQTT are also not supported. Quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', 'A digital shadow is characterized by “an automated one-way data flow... a digital shadow functions as a passive entity”'.

Accurately describes the dual encoder setup (motor and secondary/output shaft) and current sensors. It correctly captures the data flow via MQTT topics and the purpose of the arrangement (reducing backlash and positional uncertainty). This is well-supported by the source text.

The extraction correctly identifies the high-level purpose of comparing simulated versus measured data. However, it invents specific validation methods ('root-mean-square error metric'), uncertainty sources ('encoder quantization', 'MQTT network latency'), and procedural details ('recalibration routine', 'flags parameters') that are not present in the provided text. Quotes: 'purpose... of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'.

The provided Source Documents focus on creating a digital shadow for robot motion control, friction modeling, and data integration. There is no mention of data governance, ownership policies, privacy measures, or consent management in the text.

The source text describes the modeling and control of an industrial robot, including a mention of a 'safety cell' that restricts the working range. Thus, the description 'Not in Document' is correct.
